sentences.text: Well, of course, we actually went to the Dutch embassy, too, and experienced the same misery, so to speak.
Because there were always long lines.
And of course, as you know, quite a few German Jews did go emigrate to Holland, including the, Anne Frank's family.
And then we tried Belgium and even England, but it was absolutely hopeless.
And then somehow, my mother got hold of-- heard of a woman, Berlin, a German Quaker woman, who was very much interested in getting Jews out of Germany.
Her name was Mrs.
Elizabeth Landmann, Frau Elizabeth Landmann.
And she visited my mother one day.
I think it was either December 1938 or January 1939.
And she suggested that I should I join a Kindertransport, a children's transport.

These children's transport, transports started immediately after the Kristallnacht and lasted for about 11 months altogether, saving approximately 10,000 Jewish children from Nazi Germany and Austria.
And she suggested that I put my name down, and that if I was lucky, I could emigrate without a passport.
No, she was sent by somebody.
I do not remember or do not know who sent her, but she appeared one day at our doorstep.
And we simply asked her what she wanted.
And she explained.
And she said, I heard from somebody so-and-so that your son is trying to get out and your daughter, too.
But when she heard that my daughter was 10 years older than I was--
I'm so sorry.
I'm so sorry.
Yeah, my, my sister Anne Marie was 10 years older than I.
But she no longer qualified to be a member of the Kindertransport.
But since I was still 16 years old at the time, and you could be a, a member of the Kindertransport until the age of 16, she said she would try everything to get me out as quickly as possible.
And within four or six weeks, I finally got my number and could emigrate to England.
I think either 16 or 17.
Well, how Mrs.
Landmann heard about us, I do not know.
After she told us, she made the arrangements, apparently, with an organization at the British embassy in Berlin that handled these Kindertransports.

And I had to fill out or my mother had to fill out certain questionnaires about me and about my religion, when I was born, where I was born, and that she would be willing to release me, even if she would never see me again.
So as I said, all of this helped a great deal.
And I did not have a passport.
In fact, no Jews had passports, except what they call [GERMAN] or stateless passports.
So I could not have emigrated anymore to any other country in 1939.
But the Kindertransports did not need a passport.
Do you recall how long these arrangements took?
Well, as I said, we heard from Mrs.
Landmann the first time either in December of '38 or January of '39.
And by February the 8th, I was already out.
So it could only have taken six weeks or eight weeks at the very most.
Well, he was very much in agreement because he realized that Hitler will be there for good, that you simply couldn't overthrow him by that time.
He had annexed Austria and was more powerful than ever.
And so even though he had had a stroke and he was mentally [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] active again, he realized, too, that he probably would never see me again.
But he said the only way I could save my life is for you to emigrate to England.
So he really was very much in favor of my leaving him, too.
Well, first of all, the packing of my personal belongings only probably took about one day because I could only take a suitcase weighing 25 pounds with me.
That's all we could take with us.

It was very sad and yet happy at the same time because my mother knew it would save my life, since many-- especially during the Kristallnacht-- many Jewish boys, even as old as 15 and 16, were already arrested and sent to concentration camps.
So they realized it was only a matter of time before it would be my turn.
And so in that respect, of course, they were very happy that I could make it, could make it to freedom.
But losing my mother was the greatest blow of my life, and my sister, too.
Because I lived with them ever since my parents were divorced.
And it was, to me, waiting at the [GERMAN], the station in Berlin on the February the 8th, 1939, was a horrible experience.
Even though I knew it would save my life, I was equally sure probably that I would never see them alive again.
And the same, of course, applies to my father.
But of course, I was not quite as close to him as to my mother and sister.
The, the day before I left, I said goodbye to them.
I visited them on the [GERMAN].
And both of them were dear people-- I have to be absolutely honest about it-- even my stepmother, even though I never referred to her as Stepmother or Stiefmutter, but as Tante, as Aunt, Tante Ida.
But since she was a, a really decent woman, I had harbored no ill feelings toward her.
And I was very, very sad to leave both of them, too, especially my father, since he had that stroke and was completely lame on one side.
But it had to be done.
And I remember him standing on the balcony and waving to me as I left.
And that balcony still exists.
In fact, I was standing on it about two years ago.
Well, as I said, my sister was 10 years older than I was.
And there was nothing that could be done, because she was considered an adult, just like my mother.

And even though they put their names down on several lists to emigrate, as I said, to France, Belgium, Holland, England, the United States, the waiting period was a tremendous number of years, as far as the United States was concerned, I think eight years in 1938.
So they said, yes, your number will be up in 1946.
That's when you will be able to emigrate.
Of course, by that time, my mother had been gassed in Auschwitz.
But while you were going away, were you aware of any plans afoot to help your sister to survive or not?
You left just leaving your mother and sister, knowing that they are just going to be--
Well, since my-- you, you are absolutely right with that question.
But since my sister was considered an adult, just like my mother, since she was over 21, there was no difference between taking care of either her or my mother.
And nothing could be done.
They had to wait many years either to come to the United States, to England, or any other part.
Even if they had emigrated to Holland or Belgium, it would have done them very little good, as in the case of Anne Frank's family, who were all arrested later.
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
It was not-- as far as discussions are, are concerned, it was always a member of two parties because my father lived with his second wife, and I lived with my mother and sister.
So my father and my mother never even got, ever got together to discuss that.
There-- I remember about 1936, a Jewish boy friend of mine, a school friend of mine, Klaus Norden, he emigrated to the United States with his family.
He was one of the smart ones.
But you have to also realize that at the time, even though there was a, a discrimination against the Jews, we never thought it would end up in a prosecution and gas chambers.
They thought, either-- like many hundreds of thousands of German Jews, they thought well, Hitler wouldn't last much longer because he's been ostracized by the rest of the world.
And they simply felt even if he would last, it couldn't get much worse.
So they would have to-- probably couldn't practice their trade anymore, but they could still get by.
And most of them did not even try very hard to emigrate, unfortunately, my family amongst them.
And as far as my father was concerned, when he had the stroke in 1937, there was no way for him to-- for any country to accept him.
My mother tried, and that I remember.
Once we went to the American consulate.
It was very late already, probably '38.
And they told her that she would have to wait about eight years, so-- but by that time it was too late.
Well, the things we could take whatever we wanted to, as long as the suitcase didn't weigh more than 25 pounds.
I remember it happened to be on February the 8th, 1939.
We got up at about 5:00 in the morning because we had to be at the station at 7:00 in the morning.
And it was bitterly cold, bitterly cold winter's day.
And I remember dressing.
Of course, I had my own room, and my sister and my mother had their own bedroom.
And then we had breakfast.
We didn't say much.
And I noticed that my sister and my mother were crying, but we had the breakfast.
And I will never forget these tears.
And all we could do then is-- we hugged each other at home and hoped that we would meet again.
But of course, we didn't realize that World War II would start seven months later.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As I said, my mother also tried later to go to England, but the-- my sister, too-- but the quota was so oversubscribed there was no chance whatsoever.
But on February the 8th, we had no chance whatsoever.
We had no-- we, we could hardly talk.
There was nothing to discuss anymore-- I mean, just the usual maternal warnings-- be careful when you cross the street and never speak with your mouth full and stuff like that.
But generally speaking, it was not very eventful or melodramatic conversation.
The only thing is that when we hugged at the [GERMAN], at the station and said goodbye to each other, that all three of us cried.
And I remember walking down a long hallway and turning around once more and my sister and my mother waving to me in their winter coats.
And that was last I saw of my mother.
But of course, my sister was saved.
And I saw her later, long after the war again, in the United States.
Yeah, the Kindertransport-- there probably were 50 or 60 boys and girls.
They were my age and younger.
The youngest probably was around four or five years old.
And we took the train to the Dutch border.
While we waited on the German side, we had SS troops surrounding our compartments so that none of us could escape, as if we wanted to escape back into Nazi Germany.
No.
As far as I recall-- because the compartments-- we had our own compartment.
There are usually six or eight people per compartment.
And there were just boys and girls in there.
So there was nobody in my personal compartment.
I cannot remember today whether the entire lot of 60 or 70 boys and girls-- whether there was a supervisor.
Maybe there was.
But I simply cannot remember, because we were put on the train.
And the train went to Holland.
Once we reached the Dutch border and crossed into Holland, we started singing.
We started laughing.
Because the SS had left.
And it was freedom for us.
We had-- as far as I remember, my mother had made some sandwiches for me.
Because she didn't know whether we would get some.
And by the time we reached Holland, I had finished the sandwiches.
And then on the Dutch border, they had some-- as far as I recall-- some volunteer Red Cross people, who gave us more food.
No, I do not remember the city that we came to in Holland.

The only thing I remember is that we went through Holland to a place called Hoek van Holland, from which we embarked directly onto the vessel, a ferry, which took us then overnight to Harwich, in London--
--in England.
Oh, it must have been about six hours or so, five or six hours.
It's all within one day, exactly.
It happens-- we actually crossed the channel of the ocean to reach Harwich at night.
So we slept during the night.
But it was very, very stormy at the time in February.
And most of us got seasick, including myself.
In Harwich, we were put on a train, virtually all of us.
Because nobody stayed at Harwich, obviously.
That was just a, a port.
We were put on a train to London.
And nothing terribly exciting happened on the train, or nothing that stays on my mind.
But when I arrived in London, I-- everybody got off the train, of course.
And virtually everybody was met by somebody, a sponsor or whoever was responsible for that person.
We all wore little signs, giving our names and where we came from and our age.
And finally, everybody was taken care of except me.
I was standing there all by myself, speaking virtually no English.
It was bitterly cold.
And I didn't know what to do.
And I just sat there on the empty platform, and the train left again.
And I think I, I cried a little bit.
Because I thought, here I am in a foreign country.
I missed my mother like mad.
I said, I don't care about the Nazis as long as I could be with my mother and my sister again.
But finally, after about 10 minutes, somebody appeared and took me in a taxi to another station.
I can't remember the name of the station.
But I do remember we passed Buckingham Palace in the fog.
It's the first time I saw Buckingham Palace.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then finally I was put on this tiny little train.
And they put another sign up here that I should get out at that-and-that stop and-- in Dorset, which is a county in the south of England.
And it was probably a four- or five-hour trip.
And I hadn't had anything to eat since the morning.
And finally, I tried to speak to somebody in my compartment.
Where is this?
And he says, one more hour.
So finally, I reach the place.
By that time it was probably 5:00 or 6:00 in the afternoon, quite dark already.
And I disembarked.
At that time, did you understand or speak any English whatsoever?
Very little.
I had about half a year of English in school, but it was really more like pidgin English.
I mean, the door is open.
The table is covered, and so on.
It didn't really help me much.

And of course, we-- when I met my first English people, I had no way of communicating with them, because the English accent, as you know, is completely different from the broader American accent, especially when they have so many different kinds of-- southern, where I moved to, and the Cockney accent.
When we first were met, everything was Cockney around us, which is hardly intelligible, even among the general London population.
But when I first arrived in Dorset, and Mrs.
Atkinson, who was my sponsor in Dorset, at least, met me, I realized that I could never learn English.
It just was impossible.
She spoke to me nonstop.
She was a lovely woman, wonderful woman.
And I just nodded.
And she thought I understood everything.
But it really didn't help me very much.
Well, it was actually the first night.
As I said, we arrived pretty late in Dorset.
I do remember that I was given some apple juice, some cider.
I was really thirsty.
I was more thirsty than hungry for some reason, even though it was winter.
And very shortly after that I-- oh, yes, I remember something that happened in my room.
She brought up a tray with tea, wonderful English hot tea and little cookies and mustard and so on.
And there was a little plate.
And I thought it was sugar.
And I took three spoonfuls of that into my tea, not realizing it was salt.
And I drank the tea.
And I couldn't imagine how anybody could like English tea.
It tasted absolutely horrible, and-- but I was so thirsty that I simply swallowed it.

And it must have done something to me, because-- I don't know if it was just the experience of not having had any real sleep and the excitement of leaving my mother and my sister or whatever-- I went to bed, and I slept about 36 or 38 hours nonstop.
They couldn't wake me.
But when they woke me, of course, I had a ravenous appetite, and even a more ravenous thirst, because the salt had started to work on me after those 36 hours.
And then I had breakfast, my first breakfast with them.
And they were very nice, Mr. and Mrs.
Atkinson.
They bred English hunting dogs.
They had about 50 of these huge hunting dogs who love children, but they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were really my companion, so to speak.
Because somehow I could speak to them.
I couldn't speak to Mr. and Mrs.
Atkinson, since they only answered-- spoke to me in English.
And they also had three monkeys, little monkeys in a huge cage, trained monkeys.
And I was always permitted to go into the cage and feed them.
And I remember the second or third day, when I went in, I felt so lonely that I broke down and started crying.
And the monkeys hopped on my lap and on my shoulders to warm me, as if they wanted to warm me.
Or they must have realized somehow that I was sad.
I was a boy, after all.
And they just-- that was-- maybe they wanted to get warm, too.
I don't know.
But they warmed me.
And I'll never forget the occasion when they jumped on me and kept me warm.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes, absolutely, since they insisted that I have to speak English to them and listen to them.
And then gradually, they began to speak slower.
They realized that I hardly had any knowledge of English.
And within about-- I stayed there for about six, seven, or eight weeks.
I cannot remember exactly how long.
By the time that I left them, I could speak.
I could get by, at least.
And then one day, they said, we are now going to take you to your school.
You're going to sleep in that school, to stay in that school, and that will be your home until your vacation.
So I asked them, will you actually-- are you my sponsors?
Will I come back to you when I have my summer vacation?
They said no.
We will probably never meet again.
Now, unfortunately, we never did meet again.
And even when I was still in England, both Mr. and Mrs.
Atkinson died of cancer.
But they were wonderful people.
I will always love them and always remember them.
But the first day-- it happened to be a Sunday night when I arrived in school.
And the-- I remember I was taken by the headmaster, Dr.
Joad, who was waiting for me outside-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taken by him to the main meeting room for the entire school.
And as I entered with the headmaster, everybody kept staring at me.
There were about 400 students there, which gave me tremendous kind of stage fright.
And the-- Dr.
Joad introduced me to the school.
And--
I can't remember, because he spoke in English.
He just took me by the hand, and we had to stand in front of the school.
And he said something.
I heard my name being mentioned.
What he said, I have no idea.
But the entire school rose and went like this and sat down again.
It was almost like a Japanese cadet school.
But in any case, then we saw a movie with somebody called Will Hays, a horrible movie.
And I never forget it, because it was not only one of the worst movies I've ever seen, but I felt terribly lonely.
And I said, how am I going to get to communicate with everybody if I don't speak any or hardly any English?
Where was that school, would you please tell us?
That was in-- outside Blandford, Dorset, on the south coast, not too far from Dorchester, where the Atkinsons lived.
But it was a beautiful school.
It used to be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the home of a duke until about 1928, when it was converted into a college, very much like Eton and Harrow.
And it was a magnificent school.
Bryanston College, Bryanston School or Bryanston College in Blandford.
And I only remember that it was bitterly cold.
And after the movie, I didn't know where to go.
Somebody took me by the hand to my bedroom, which I shared with two other students.
And throughout the entire night, I shivered.
That I remember, because I only had one blanket.
And they had the window open.
It must have been about 10 below zero.
But I didn't only shiver from the cold but from the fear of what is ahead of me and missing my parents, my mother, my sister, my father.
But then the next day started.
And I had nowhere to go after breakfast.
The breakfast-- at least, they had a wonderful bowl of porridge.
And after that, I was taken by the hand, by my hand again, to a classroom.
Didn't understand a word or very little, at least, because the teacher spoke in a, a sort of a southern accent.
But gradually I got into it and got used to my school life at Bryanston.
Well, it, it's different from the American school.
We had all the subjects.
We had German, French, English, mathematics, science, whatever.
Whatever they taught in Germany, [? too, ?] in [GERMAN] or in gymnasiums, it was the same in the college.
